item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Selective Hydrogenation of Nitriles to Secondary Imines Using a Pincer Manganese Complex
item.dc:description:
An earth‐abundant manganese‐based pincer complexcatalyzed the selective hydrogenation of nitriles to secondary imines. The reaction proceeds in the presence of catalytic base under H2 pressure generating NH3 as byproduct.
An earth‐abundant manganese‐based pincer phosphine complex (Ph‐PNP)Mn(Br)(CO)2, (Ph‐PNP = bis(2‐(diphenylphosphaneyl)ethyl)amine) Mn‐1 catalyzed selective hydrogenation of nitriles to secondary imines is reported. The reaction proceeds in the presence of catalytic base under H2 pressure generating NH3 as a byproduct. An optimized loading of 2 mol% Mn‐1, 20 mol% KOtBu revealed hydrogenation of benzonitrile to corresponding N‐benzylidenenbenzylamine in 92% yield after 24 h at 140°C in toluene under 40 bar H2. A wide range of substituted benzonitriles could be hydrogenated to corresponding secondary imines in good to excellent yields under the reaction conditions. The substrate scope displayed promising outcome for heteroaromatic nitriles but poor yields for aliphatic nitrile hydrogenation. Notably, the reaction does not lead to further hydrogenation of intermediate imines to form secondary amines. Stoichiometric evidences and computational studies indicate possible mechanism involving metal–ligand cooperative H2 activation and subsequent transfer of H2 to unsaturated nitrile moiety.
item.content:encoded:
An earth-abundant manganese-based pincer complexcatalyzed the selective hydrogenation of nitriles to secondary imines. The reaction proceeds in the presence of catalytic base under H2 pressure generating NH3 as byproduct.


An earth-abundant manganese-based pincer phosphine complex (Ph-PNP)Mn(Br)(CO)2, (Ph-PNP = bis(2-(diphenylphosphaneyl)ethyl)amine) Mn-1 catalyzed selective hydrogenation of nitriles to secondary imines is reported. The reaction proceeds in the presence of catalytic base under H2 pressure generating NH3 as a byproduct. An optimized loading of 2 mol% Mn-1, 20 mol% KO t Bu revealed hydrogenation of benzonitrile to corresponding N-benzylidenenbenzylamine in 92% yield after 24 h at 140°C in toluene under 40 bar H2. A wide range of substituted benzonitriles could be hydrogenated to corresponding secondary imines in good to excellent yields under the reaction conditions. The substrate scope displayed promising outcome for heteroaromatic nitriles but poor yields for aliphatic nitrile hydrogenation. Notably, the reaction does not lead to further hydrogenation of intermediate imines to form secondary amines. Stoichiometric evidences and computational studies indicate possible mechanism involving metal–ligand cooperative H2 activation and subsequent transfer of H2 to unsaturated nitrile moiety.
item.dc:creator: Sachin Jalwal, Aman Anand, Dhirajkumar Jitendrabhai Patanvadiya, Manikandan Paranjothy, Subrata Chakraborty
item.dc:title: Selective Hydrogenation of Nitriles to Secondary Imines Using a Pincer Manganese Complex

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Bio‐Photocatalytic Green Synthesis of 2‐Arylbenzoxazoles from Toluene
item.dc:description:
A green and one‐pot synthesis of 2‐arylbenzoxazoles is achieved via a dual‐enzyme photocatalytic cascade. Starting from toluene, the process combines lipase and hemoglobin catalysis in aqueous medium at room temperature, efficiently assembling drug‐like molecules from a simple feedstock with high atom economy and minimal environmental impact.
2‐Arylbenzoxazoles were synthesized efficiently using an innovative, environmentally friendly, and mild system that integrates biocatalysis and photocatalysis. This approach employs sodium anthraquinone sulfonate (SAS) as a photosensitive agent within the same vessel, utilizing water as the solvent. This method effectively converts toluene, an industrial raw material, into the desired final products, which are drug backbone benzoxazole derivatives. The intermediates, benzaldoximes, generated in the initial step are fully utilized in the subsequent stages, and the plausible reaction mechanism is also discussed. This process, along with its satisfactory results, enhances the range of photocatalytic applications, thereby broadening the scope of unnatural reactions catalyzed by two commercially available proteins, porcine pancreas lipase (PPL) and hemoglobin.
item.content:encoded:
A green and one-pot synthesis of 2-arylbenzoxazoles is achieved via a dual-enzyme photocatalytic cascade. Starting from toluene, the process combines lipase and hemoglobin catalysis in aqueous medium at room temperature, efficiently assembling drug-like molecules from a simple feedstock with high atom economy and minimal environmental impact.


2-Arylbenzoxazoles were synthesized efficiently using an innovative, environmentally friendly, and mild system that integrates biocatalysis and photocatalysis. This approach employs sodium anthraquinone sulfonate (SAS) as a photosensitive agent within the same vessel, utilizing water as the solvent. This method effectively converts toluene, an industrial raw material, into the desired final products, which are drug backbone benzoxazole derivatives. The intermediates, benzaldoximes, generated in the initial step are fully utilized in the subsequent stages, and the plausible reaction mechanism is also discussed. This process, along with its satisfactory results, enhances the range of photocatalytic applications, thereby broadening the scope of unnatural reactions catalyzed by two commercially available proteins, porcine pancreas lipase (PPL) and hemoglobin.

item.dc:title: Bio‐Photocatalytic Green Synthesis of 2‐Arylbenzoxazoles from Toluene

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Gold(I)‐Catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) Cross‐Coupling: Access to Alkynylated Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives
item.dc:description:
An Au(I)‐catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) cross‐coupling, unsuccessful under photosensitized conditions for vinylgold(I) complexes, is achieved using MeDalPhosAuCl under thermal conditions, giving alkynylated tetrahydrofuran derivatives. The reaction proceeds via an Au(III) intermediate formed by oxidative addition of an alkynyl iodide.
This study explores the possibility of operating a previously unsuccessful Au(I)‐catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) cross‐coupling. While the photosensitization conditions previously used for the C(sp2)–C(sp) cross‐coupling reaction with vinylgold(I) intermediates were not successful with alkylgold(I) complexes, the use of the MeDalPhosAuCl complex as a catalyst under thermal conditions provided moderate to good yields of C(sp3)‐alkynylated adducts, particularly alkynylated tetrahydrofuran derivatives. Mechanistic studies revealed the formation of an Au(III) intermediate, resulting from oxidative addition of the alkynyl iodide partner onto the Au(I) complex bearing the hemilabile MeDalPhos ligand.
item.content:encoded:

The reaction proceeds via an Au(III) intermediate formed by oxidative addition of an alkynyl iodide.


This study explores the possibility of operating a previously unsuccessful Au(I)-catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) cross-coupling. While the photosensitization conditions previously used for the C(sp2)–C(sp) cross-coupling reaction with vinylgold(I) intermediates were not successful with alkylgold(I) complexes, the use of the MeDalPhosAuCl complex as a catalyst under thermal conditions provided moderate to good yields of C(sp3)-alkynylated adducts, particularly alkynylated tetrahydrofuran derivatives. Mechanistic studies revealed the formation of an Au(III) intermediate, resulting from oxidative addition of the alkynyl iodide partner onto the Au(I) complex bearing the hemilabile MeDalPhos ligand.

item.dc:title: Gold(I)‐Catalyzed C(sp3)–C(sp) Cross‐Coupling: Access to Alkynylated Tetrahydrofuran Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced Amine‐Mediated Alkylpyridylation of Styrenes
item.dc:description:
A photoinduced alkylpyridylation of styrenes has been developed under catalyst‐free conditions using alkyl iodides and substituted 4‐cyanopyridines as a source of alkyl and pyridyl precursors, respectively. Mechanistic and DFT studies suggest that the amine plays a crucial role in generating the corresponding alkyl radical and aryl radical anion via halogen bonding and an EDA‐initiated SET mechanism, respectively.
Herein, we report the photoinduced, catalyst‐free alkylpyridylation of styrenes to access privileged 1,1‐diarylalkane scaffolds. Unactivated alkyl iodides and substituted 4‐cyanopyridines serve as the alkyl and pyridyl precursors, respectively. Preliminary mechanistic and computational studies suggest that the amine plays a crucial role in generating the corresponding alkyl radical and aryl radical anion, possibly via halogen bonding and EDA‐initiated SET mechanisms, respectively. The method offers several advantages, including mild reaction conditions, wide substrate compatibility, and suitability for late‐stage modification of pharmaceutical compounds.

Mechanistic and DFT studies suggest that the amine plays a crucial role in generating the corresponding alkyl radical and aryl radical anion via halogen bonding and an EDA-initiated SET mechanism, respectively.

Unactivated alkyl iodides and substituted 4-cyanopyridines serve as the alkyl and pyridyl precursors, respectively. Preliminary mechanistic and computational studies suggest that the amine plays a crucial role in generating the corresponding alkyl radical and aryl radical anion, possibly via halogen bonding and EDA-initiated SET mechanisms, respectively. The method offers several advantages, including mild reaction conditions, wide substrate compatibility, and suitability for late-stage modification of pharmaceutical compounds.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Domino Ring‐Opening Cyclization of Aziridines with Isatogens: A Stereoselective Synthetic Route to Fluorescent Tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3‐a]indolones
item.dc:description:
A Cu(I)‐catalysed domino ring‐opening cyclisation (DROC) enables rapid assembly of fluorescent tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3‐a]indolones from activated aziridines and isatogens with excellent regio‐, diastereo‐, and enantioselectivity. A one‐pot nitroalkyne cycloisomerisation/DROC sequence highlights the versatility, while optoelectronic properties reveal strong emission, solvatochromism, and high quantum efficiency.
A highly regio‐ and stereoselective Cu(I)‐catalysed domino ring‐opening cyclisation (DROC) of activated aziridines with isatogens has been developed, providing access to tricyclic fluorescent tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3‐a]indolones bearing a substituted quaternary carbon center. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions, delivering products in up to 99% yield with excellent diastereo‐ (up to 99% dr) and enantioselectivity (99% ee for non‐racemic aziridines). A representative one‐pot sequence merging nitroalkyne cycloisomerisation with DROC of aziridines further demonstrates the versatility of this methodology. The resulting heterocycles display promising photophysical properties, including UV–visible absorption, fluorescence emission, and lifetime measurements, as well as pronounced solvatochromism. Cyclic voltammetry was employed to estimate electrochemical band gaps and HOMO–LUMO energy levels, and the compounds exhibit high fluorescence efficiency (quantum yields up to 0.6), highlighting their potential for opto‐electronic applications.
item.content:encoded:
A Cu(I)-catalysed domino ring-opening cyclisation (DROC) enables rapid assembly of fluorescent tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3-a]indolones from activated aziridines and isatogens with excellent regio-, diastereo-, and enantioselectivity. A one-pot nitroalkyne cycloisomerisation/DROC sequence highlights the versatility, while optoelectronic properties reveal strong emission, solvatochromism, and high quantum efficiency.


A highly regio- and stereoselective Cu(I)-catalysed domino ring-opening cyclisation (DROC) of activated aziridines with isatogens has been developed, providing access to tricyclic fluorescent tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3-a]indolones bearing a substituted quaternary carbon center. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions, delivering products in up to 99% yield with excellent diastereo- (up to 99% dr) and enantioselectivity (99% ee for non-racemic aziridines). A representative one-pot sequence merging nitroalkyne cycloisomerisation with DROC of aziridines further demonstrates the versatility of this methodology. The resulting heterocycles display promising photophysical properties, including UV–visible absorption, fluorescence emission, and lifetime measurements, as well as pronounced solvatochromism. Cyclic voltammetry was employed to estimate electrochemical band gaps and HOMO–LUMO energy levels, and the compounds exhibit high fluorescence efficiency (quantum yields up to 0.6), highlighting their potential for opto-electronic applications.

item.dc:title: Domino Ring‐Opening Cyclization of Aziridines with Isatogens: A Stereoselective Synthetic Route to Fluorescent Tetrahydrooxadiazino[2,3‐a]indolones

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Exploring Diverse Glycone Preferences of Aspergillus rutinosidases
item.dc:description:
Rutinosidases from Aspergillus niger (AnRut) and A. oryzae (AoRut) catalyze hydrolysis and transglycosylation with non‐natural glycosides as donors, enabling glycosylation of alcoholic and phenolic acceptors. AnRut exhibits broad glycone promiscuity, while AoRut has a narrower substrate scope. Both enzymes produce donor self‐condensation products, expanding the synthetic potential of fungal rutinosidases.
Fungal rutinosidases are retaining diglycosidases that release rutinose from flavonoid glycosides, catalyzing valuable transglycosylation reactions. We systematically compare the substrate specificity and synthetic capabilities of two structurally characterized fungal rutinosidases from Aspergillus niger (AnRut) and A. oryzae (AoRut), across a panel of natural, modified, and non‐natural glycosides. AnRut displays remarkable glycone promiscuity, efficiently hydrolyzing not only rutinosides and β‐glucopyranosides but also β‐galactopyranosides, β‐xylopyranosides, and α‐L‐arabinopyranosides. AnRut also processes diglycosides, such as rutinosides and isomaltosides. It catalyzes transglycosylations with non‐natural glycosides as donors, glycosylating alcoholic and phenolic acceptors, including transfer of β‐D‐galacto‐, β‐D‐xylo‐, and α‐L‐arabinopyranosyl residues. In contrast, AoRut exhibits a narrower substrate range: it recognizes rutinosides and selected monoglycosides as substrates but does not hydrolyze most other diglycosides. Unexpectedly, AoRut catalyzes efficient transglycosylation using p‐nitrophenyl β‐D‐galactopyranoside and pNP‐α‐L‐arabinopyranoside as donors, producing novel β‐galacto‐ and α‐L‐arabinopyranosides of various phenolic acceptors. Both enzymes also catalyze donor self‐condensation products, such as pNP‐β‐D‐glucopyranosyl‐(1→3)‐β‐D‐glucopyranoside and pNP‐β‐D‐xylopyranosyl‐(1→3)‐β‐D‐xylopyranoside and pNP‐β‐D‐xylopyranosyl‐(1→2)‐β‐D‐xylopyranoside (only AoRut). Notably, this is the first reported case of a rutinosidase glycosylating a sugar acceptor. These findings reveal distinct glycone preferences and mechanistic features of the two enzymes and significantly expand the repertoire of glycosides accessible through fungal rutinosidases, highlighting their utility for synthesizing novel carbohydrate derivatives.

AnRut exhibits broad glycone promiscuity, while AoRut has a narrower substrate scope. Both enzymes produce donor self-condensation products, expanding the synthetic potential of fungal rutinosidases.

Both enzymes also catalyze donor self-condensation products, such as pNP-β-D-glucopyranosyl-(1→3)-β-D-glucopyranoside and pNP-β-D-xylopyranosyl-(1→3)-β-D-xylopyranoside and pNP-β-D-xylopyranosyl-(1→2)-β-D-xylopyranoside (only AoRut). item.title: Synergetic Bifunctional Effect of the Solvent Leading to External Base‐Free Neutral Construction of Unsymmetrical Thioethers From Thiols and Organohalides in N,N‐Dimethylformamide
item.dc:description:
Without adding any external base, solvent DMF is found able to effectively promote the substitution reactions of thiols and various organohalides to afford the useful unsymmetrical thioethers under neutral and greener conditions. Studies show that DMF may have a synergetic bifunctional effect in the reactions working as both the best solvent and an excellent organic base.
Different to other solvents, a unique promoting effect of DMF is observed in the aromatic nucleophilic substitution reaction of heteroaryl halides and thiols. Thus, by using DMF as the solvent, a greener method can be developed for direct neutral synthesis of heteroaryl thioethers without adding any external base into the reaction. This method can also be extended to alkyl and electron‐deficient aryl halides, providing a new way for external base‐free neutral construction of the useful unsymmetrical thioethers. Control reactions revealed that DMF functions more than a good solvent in the reaction, i.e., a synergetic bifunctional effect of DMF working as both the best solvent and an excellent organic base can eventually promote the substitution reactions of thiols and organohalides effectively without using any external base.
item.content:encoded:
Without adding any external base, solvent DMF is found able to effectively promote the substitution reactions of thiols and various organohalides to afford the useful unsymmetrical thioethers under neutral and greener conditions. Studies show that DMF may have a synergetic bifunctional effect in the reactions working as both the best solvent and an excellent organic base.


Different to other solvents, a unique promoting effect of DMF is observed in the aromatic nucleophilic substitution reaction of heteroaryl halides and thiols. Thus, by using DMF as the solvent, a greener method can be developed for direct neutral synthesis of heteroaryl thioethers without adding any external base into the reaction. This method can also be extended to alkyl and electron-deficient aryl halides, providing a new way for external base-free neutral construction of the useful unsymmetrical thioethers. Control reactions revealed that DMF functions more than a good solvent in the reaction, i.e., a synergetic bifunctional effect of DMF working as both the best solvent and an excellent organic base can eventually promote the substitution reactions of thiols and organohalides effectively without using any external base.
item.dc:creator: Mengli Li, Guoshun Jin, Jianhui chen, Qing Xu, Li‐Biao Han
item.dc:title: Synergetic Bifunctional Effect of the Solvent Leading to External Base‐Free Neutral Construction of Unsymmetrical Thioethers From Thiols and Organohalides in N,N‐Dimethylformamide

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: N‐Amidinyl‐Benzodiazaborines as Novel Boron‐Based Scaffolds: Synthesis and Application in Retromer Stabilization
item.dc:description:
In this work, we successfully synthesized a library of novel N‐amidinyl‐benzodiazaborines, showing how these heteroaryl guanyl hydrazones are able to stabilize the retromer, a protein complex involved in the disposal of misfolded and aggregated proteins, whose malfunction is observed in multiple neurodegenerative diseases.
Diazaborines, a class of boron‐containing compounds known for their antibacterial activity, are synthesized from 2‐formylphenylboronic acid and hydrazines. Yet, the use of aminoguanidine has never been explored; thus, no examples of N‐amidinyl‐benzodiazaborines have been reported to date. Herein, we computationally and experimentally investigated the synthesis and characterization of novel N‐amidinyl‐benzodiazaborines, showing how these heteroaryl guanyl hydrazones are able to stabilize the retromer, a protein complex whose malfunction impairs the disposal of neurotoxic protein aggregates in neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease (PD) and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
item.content:encoded:
In this work, we successfully synthesized a library of novel N-amidinyl-benzodiazaborines, showing how these heteroaryl guanyl hydrazones are able to stabilize the retromer, a protein complex involved in the disposal of misfolded and aggregated proteins, whose malfunction is observed in multiple neurodegenerative diseases.


Diazaborines, a class of boron-containing compounds known for their antibacterial activity, are synthesized from 2-formylphenylboronic acid and hydrazines. Yet, the use of aminoguanidine has never been explored; thus, no examples of N-amidinyl-benzodiazaborines have been reported to date. Herein, we computationally and experimentally investigated the synthesis and characterization of novel N-amidinyl-benzodiazaborines, showing how these heteroaryl guanyl hydrazones are able to stabilize the retromer, a protein complex whose malfunction impairs the disposal of neurotoxic protein aggregates in neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease (PD) and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
item.dc:creator: Alessandro Dimasi, Arianna Montoli, Michele Buonaguro, Leonardo Maiorana, Giovanni Macetti, Eleonora Colombo, Luca Muzio, Greta Donati, Daniele Passarella, Pierfausto Seneci, Valerio Fasano
